one_line_takeaway: By forcing an LLM to always allege a vulnerability and then fine-tuning a different LLM to tell real reports from hallucinated ones, HALURust turns hallucination into a signal and reaches ~77% F1 on real-world Rust CVEs, ~10% over prior methods.
key_finding: The report-based, hallucination-detection approach substantially beats both code-based fine-tuning and prior real-world Rust vulnerability detectors. Best configuration (CO-STAR + Llama3) reaches peak accuracy 77.4% and peak F1 77.3% (average 74.5% acc / 74.8% F1), roughly a 10% improvement over 7 existing methods (which sat at 45-55% accuracy in real-world Rust). Feeding LLM-generated reports instead of raw source into fine-tuning improved detection by ~20% over code-based fine-tuning. Prompt structure matters strongly: TO prompts barely beat random (40-55%), RO gave large gains (e.g., Codellama 50.3% -> 67.6% accuracy), and CO-STAR added ~7% more on average.
core_contribution: HALURust is a framework that deliberately exploits LLM hallucination to detect vulnerabilities in real-world Rust code. Its central trick: prompt an LLM to ALWAYS assume the input code contains a vulnerability and write a technical vulnerability report. For genuinely vulnerable code the report is accurate; for fixed/non-vulnerable code the model is forced to invent a flaw, producing a hallucinated report. A separate LLM is then fine-tuned to classify reports as real vs. hallucinated, which yields the vulnerable/non-vulnerable label for the underlying code. The paper's own new artifacts are: (1) a Rust vulnerability dataset built from 81 real CVE records (447 functions, 18,691 LOC, 44 CWEs, 54 programs), where each CVE yields one vulnerable sample (functions containing the flawed code) and one non-vulnerable sample (the corresponding fixed functions); and (2) the report-based, hallucination-detection detection pipeline itself. Note: the many static-analysis and formal-verification Rust tools named early (Rudra, Safedrop, MirChecker, RustBelt, Yuga, RustHorn, etc.) are cited related work, NOT this paper's contribution.
threat_model: The paper targets detection of software vulnerabilities in real-world Rust applications, framed around CVE/CWE records (442 Rust CVEs since 2018 cited as motivation). The security stake is that Rust's ownership/borrowing safety guarantees can be bypassed via 'unsafe' blocks and logic errors, producing exploitable flaws (buffer overflow, use-after-free, race conditions, etc.). This is a defensive detection tool, not an adversarial attack paper; there is no active adversary manipulating the model. The relevant CWE families the paper organizes its dataset into are Memory Safety, Input Validation, Concurrency, and Security Handling.
limitations: (1) Small dataset (only 81 CVEs, one positive + one negative each; 44 CWEs many with a single sample) which the authors admit limits model stability and per-type accuracy. (2) Data-memorization/leakage risk: the CVEs are public and may be in LLM training data; the authors argue prompt-dependent variance shows the effect is not pure memorization, but this is not conclusive. (3) Report-quality risk: only CO-STAR reports were manually verified for errors; TO and RO reports were not checked. (4) Weak generalization to contrasting or dissimilar CWEs, e.g., it may catch CWE-190 (integer overflow) but misclassify CWE-191 (integer underflow), and it fails on web (CWE-079) and cryptographic (CWE-347) vulnerabilities unlike its training set. Unseen-CWE accuracy (~63%) is below overall. (5) Positive/negative samples differ only by the patch, so the classifier may be learning patch-shaped surface cues rather than deep vulnerability semantics. (6) Ceiling of ~77% is still far from deployable-grade reliability.

datasets_used: Authors' own Rust CVE dataset: 81 CVE records, 447 functions, 18,691 LOC, 44 CWEs, 54 programs (each CVE -> 1 vulnerable + 1 non-vulnerable sample)
Cross-language transfer set from ref [15]: 13 most-common CWEs each for C and Java, 500 positive + 500 negative per CWE = 6,500 positive + 6,500 negative samples per language
quantitative_results: Best config CO-STAR + Llama3: peak accuracy 77.4% (avg 74.5% +2.9), peak F1 77.3% (avg 74.8% +2.5), precision 73.1%, recall 75.9%
Report-based fine-tuning improved detection by ~20% over code-based fine-tuning
TO prompts: near-random 40-55% accuracy/F1; RO example Codellama accuracy 50.3%->67.6%, F1 45.4%->69.6%; CO-STAR adds ~7% avg over RO
Ablation: zero-shot and few-shot (code or report) all <40% accuracy; code-input + fine-tuning only ~50% accuracy/F1 (~60% recall)
Unseen CWEs: 27 unseen, 17 correctly identified, 10 misclassified, ~63% accuracy
Cross-language C: accuracy 92.5-94.7%, Llama3 best F1 95.0%
Cross-language Java: accuracy 94.7-96.6%, GPT-4 best F1 96.4%
Motivation stat: 442 Rust-related CVEs reported since 2018
baselines_compared: Le et al. with ChatGPT (all 7 baselines: 45-55% accuracy, 60-70% F1 in real-world Rust)
